coredata.dc:title: Mild bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots with temperature-dependent fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Hong-Gang
Lu, Xuan
Cheng, Rui
Guo, Jiazhuang
Li, He
Wang, Cai-Feng
Chen, Su
coredata.dc:description: Bottom-up methods allowing the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) with favorable attributes are highly desirable. Herein, we report the mild bottom-up synthesis of CDs towards temperature sensing. Five kinds of CDs with blue or green fluorescence were synthesized in water or tetrahydrofuran (THF) under moderate conditions (normal pressure, 50~95 °C), with use of carbonyl compounds as carbon sources, such as acetylacetone (AA), cyclohexanone (CH), 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone (HM), glutaric dialdehyde (GD), and salicylaldehyde (SA). Typically, with use of acetylacetone (AA) as carbon sources, CDs were synthesized at 95 °C in water in the presence of zinc chloride and 1, 2-dichloroethane. The synthesis, characterizations, and optical properties of AA-derived CDs were thoroughly investigated. The resultant CDs exhibit green photoluminescence (PL) at 520 nm with absolute PL quantum yield (QY) of 21%. Moreover, the PL intensity proportionally depends on temperature in the range 150–360 K, which makes AA-derived CDs potentially useful for temperature sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mild synthesis
Bottom-up
Photoluminescence
Temperature sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone-derived carbon dots (HM-CDs), glutaric dialdehyde-derived carbon dots (GD-CDs) and salicylaldehyde-derived carbon dots (SA-CDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of AA-CDs. Inset picture: HRTEM image of AA-CDs. (b) XRD pattern of AA-CDs. (c) Raman spectrum of AA-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of AA-CDs. (e) High-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of AA-CDs. (f) High-resolution O 1s XPS spectra of AA-CDs.

Optical properties of AA-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption, and PL emission (λex = 440 nm) spectra. Inset: photographs of AA-CDs in water taken under day light and under UV light (365 nm), respectively. (b) PL emission spectra obtained from different excitation wavelengths. (c) Time-resolved PL decay curve of AA-CDs. (d) Dependence of the absolute PL QY on the reaction time.

Optical properties of CDs prepared from HM, CH, GD and SA. (a–d) UV–vis absorption, and PL emission spectra of HM-CDs (λex = 390 nm), CH-CDs (λex = 390 nm), GD-CDs (λex = 390 nm) and SA-CDS (λex = 440 nm). The inset pictures show the photographs of CDs under daylight and under UV light (365 nm). (e–h) PL emission spectra of HM-CDs, CH-CDs, GD-CDs and SA-CDs obtained from different excitation wavelengths. (i–l) The time-resolved PL decay curves of HM-CDs, CH-CDs, GD-CDs and SA-CDs.

PL emission spectra of AA-CDs in (a) water and (b) 2-methyltetrahydrofuran under variable temperature. (c) PL emission spectra obtained from 20 times of temperature cycle. (d) I/I0-temperature plots of an aqueous solution of AA-CDs under 280–360 K. (e) I/I0-temperature plots of AA-CDs in 2-methyltetrahydrofuran under 150–300 K. (f) PL intensities of AA-CDs obtained from 20 times of temperature cycle.
Schematic synthesis of CDs from different carbonyl compounds as carbon precursors.
Synthetic information and optical properties of CDs.

head.ce:title: Mild bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots with temperature-dependent fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bottom-up methods allowing the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) with favorable attributes are highly desirable. Herein, we report the mild bottom-up synthesis of CDs towards temperature sensing. Five kinds of CDs with blue or green fluorescence were synthesized in water or tetrahydrofuran (THF) under moderate conditions (normal pressure, 50~95 °C), with use of carbonyl compounds as carbon sources, such as acetylacetone (AA), cyclohexanone (CH), 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone (HM), glutaric dialdehyde (GD), and salicylaldehyde (SA). Typically, with use of acetylacetone (AA) as carbon sources, CDs were synthesized at 95 °C in water in the presence of zinc chloride and 1, 2-dichloroethane. The synthesis, characterizations, and optical properties of AA-derived CDs were thoroughly investigated. The resultant CDs exhibit green photoluminescence (PL) at 520 nm with absolute PL quantum yield (QY) of 21%. Moreover, the PL intensity proportionally depends on temperature in the range 150–360 K, which makes AA-derived CDs potentially useful for temperature sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: As a type of carbon-based zero-dimensional fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention since they were discovered in 2004 [1]. These nanomaterials have superior optical properties, accompanying with favorable attributes such as low toxicity, environmental friendliness, good biocompatibility, wide sources of raw materials, or easy surface functionalization [2–5]. Accordingly, CDs show promising application prospects in various fields including bioimaging, fluorescent display, photovoltaic cells, catalysis, medical diagnosis, metal ions detection, etc. [6–10]. To date, diverse approaches have been developed to prepare CDs, such as arc discharge [11], laser ablation [12], plasma treatments [13], electrochemical oxidation, pyrolysis [14], microwave [15] or ultrasonic methods [16]. Whereas, according to the formation nature of CDs, all of those synthesis ways could be generally classified into two categories, namely top-down and bottom-up routes. CDs are prepared from larger carbon structures such as nanotubes or graphene in the top-down routes [17], while the bottom-up routes allow the synthesis of CDs from small molecules like citric acid and amines [15]. Particularly, the bottom-up synthesis strategies of CDs have received increasing interest, benefiting from rich varieties of molecular precursors as well as abundant surface functionalization and desirable optical properties for the resultant CDs [18]. In this respect, hydrothermal/solvothermal procedures are the most commonly used bottom-up synthetic strategies currently, which require relatively high pressure and temperature usually >150 °C. Recently, convenient low-temperature synthesis of CDs by acidic oxidation has also been developed [19–21]. In view of this point, we herein devoted our effort to the exploration of bottom-up methods allowing CDs to be synthesized under mild conditions.

Temperature, as one of fundamental physical quantities, always plays as a key parameter in various physical, chemical and biological processes [22]. As such, the temperature detection is of great importance. Recently, fluorescence-based temperature sensors have been reported, including nanoparticles, rare-earth doped phosphors and organometallic complexes [23–25]. Among those reported, there are a few studies on the temperature-dependent fluorescence behavior of CDs. Macairan et al. developed a fluorescence-based ratiometric temperature sensor by CDs synthesized by microwave method, which shows a linear response as a function of temperature in the range of 5–60 °C [26]. Li et al. reported a manganese oxide doped CDs by hydrothermal method, to show reversible temperature-sensitive fluorescence in vitro in the range of 10–60 °C [27]. Despite advances, the facile synthesis of CDs with wide temperature detection window is highly desirable.

Herein, we report the new mild bottom-up synthesis of CDs, as well as their potential application in temperature detection with wide temperature sensing range (150–360 K). We synthesized five kinds of CDs from carbonyl compounds via a simple bottom-up synthesis method under mild conditions. As shown in Scheme 1 , the synthetic process was carried out using general organic reaction operation implemented under atmospheric pressure and moderate temperature (50~95 °C), circumventing the use of high pressure, high temperature, strong acid, and elaborate apparatus. Green-emissive CDs with emission peak centered at 520 nm and PL QY of 21% were synthesized by simply heating acetylacetone (AA), 1, 2-dichloroethane and zinc chloride in water at 95 °C. Similarly, multicolor-emissive CDs from blue to green were synthesized by using different carbonyl compounds as carbon sources, such as cyclohexanone (CH), 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone (HM), glutaric dialdehyde (GD), or salicylaldehyde (SA), with 1, 2-dichloroethane and indium chloride in THF at 50~90 °C. Interestingly, AA-derived CDs exhibit temperature-dependent fluorescence in the temperature range varying from 150 to 360 K, which makes them potentially useful for temperature detection. Therefore, this work contributes a mild bottom-up approach to the synthesis of CDs with favorable optical properties.

Zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 98%), indium chloride (InCl3, 99.99%), magnesium sulphate (MgSO4, 98%), tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99.9%), cyclohexanone (CH, C6H10O, 99.9%), 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone (HM, C10H12O2, 99%), glutaric dialdehyde (GD, C5H8O2, 99%), salicylaldehyde (SA, C7H6O2, 99%), acetylacetone (AA, C5H8O2, 99%), 1, 2-dichloroethane (C2H4Cl2, 99%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co, LTD. Deionized water used in this work has resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 at 25 °C. All chemicals were used without further purification.

Briefly, AA (10 mmol) and ZnCl2 (18 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of DI water in a 100 mL 3-neck round bottom flask, stirring at room temperature under nitrogen (N2) atmosphere until the solution was transparent (about 5 min). Subsequently, 1, 2-dichloroethane (0.1 mL) was added into the above solution with a needle, and then the solution was heated at 95 °C for 9 h. The as-prepared mixture was purified by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min, and the precipitate was discarded. The supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off: 500 Da) for 2 days and then freeze-dried, to afford 0.3650 g of AA-CDs (yield calculated with respect to the amount of AA: 36 wt%).

CH (10 mmol) and InCl3 (6 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of THF in a 100 mL 3-neck round bottom flask under the protection of N2 with stirring for 5 min. 1, 2-dichloroethane (0.1 mL) was injected into the above solution with a syringe. After refluxing for 24 h at 70 °C, the solution was cooled to room temperature and filtered with dichloromethane/water. Subsequently, the resultant mixture was dried with anhydrous MgSO4 and centrifugated at 8000 rpm for 15 min. After that, the supernatant was purified by dialyzing with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off: 1 k Da) for 2 days and freeze dried for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone-derived carbon dots (HM-CDs), glutaric dialdehyde-derived carbon dots (GD-CDs) and salicylaldehyde-derived carbon dots (SA-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis processes of HM-CDs, GD-CDs and SA-CDs were similar with CH-CDs just replacing the 10 mmol of CH with 10 mmol of HM, 10 mmol of GD and 10 mmol of SA, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken with JEOL JEM-2100 operated at 200 keV. The structure of obtained CDs was characterized by X-ray diﬀraction (XRD) from a D5005 X-ray diﬀractometer (Siemens AG, Munich, Germany) from 5° to 85° at a scanning speed of 5°/min. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on an ESCALAB 250 XI XPS instrument (Thermo Scientific, USA) in an ultra-high vacuum system using monochromated Al Kα (150 W, 20 eV pass energy) radiation. The spectra were recorded at 100 eV pass energy with the step size of 1.00 eV for the survey scan. The peaks were fitted and analyzed using XPSPEAK 41 software. Fourier-transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was obtained via the American Thermo Nicolet-6700 Fourier Infrared spectroscopy. The steady-state PL spectra were collected by using a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer at room temperature. The absolute PL quantum yields (QY) and Time-resolved PL decay curves were measured by an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrophotometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer Lambda 900 UV–vis spectrometer.

In this work, we report a mild bottom-up method for synthesizing CDs from the carbonyl compounds including acetylacetone (AA), cyclohexanone (CH), 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone (HM), glutaric dialdehyde (GD), and salicylaldehyde (SA), respectively, and the resultant CDs are referred to as AA-CDs, CH-CDs, HM-CDs, GD-CDs and SA-CDs, respectively. Unlike hydrothermal/solvothermal procedures, the most commonly used for bottom-up synthesis of CDs, usually requiring relatively high pressure and temperature (usually > 150 °C), this bottom-up synthesis process was implemented under atmospheric pressure and moderate temperature (50~95 °C), as shown in Scheme 1. The reaction proceeded in H2O or THF, where carbonyl compounds were utilized as carbon source, 1, 2-dichloroethane was added as additive solvent, and a Lewis acid ZnCl2 or InCl3 was employed as catalyst to promote Aldol reaction, an effective method for the construction of carbon-carbon bonds [28,29], ultimately forming CDs. Specifically, Scheme S1 illustrates the possible formation process for AA-CDs. With use of AA as carbon precursors, green-emissive CDs were synthesized by simply heating in the presence of 1, 2-dichloroethane and ZnCl2 in water at 95 °C. Similarly, HM-CDs, CH-CDs, GD-CDs and SA-CDs were synthesized from the corresponding carbonyl compounds HM, CH, GD and SA with InCl3 and 1, 2-dichloroethane in THF at 50~95 °C, to exhibit multicolor-emissive PL in the blue and green regions.

AA-CDs were chosen as the representative example to thoroughly carried out structure analyses and optical properties investigation in this work. In order to confirm the morphology of the AA-CDs, TEM characterization was performed. Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image of AA-CDs. It can be seen that the CDs are spherical without aggregation. The inset picture is the HRTEM image of CDs, showing an obvious lattice fringe. The lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction facet of graphitic carbon [30]. Figure S1 exhibits the size distribution of AA-CDs, indicating the average size of 3.97 nm. The XRD pattern of AA-CDs is shown in Fig. 1b. There are two apparent peaks at approximately 24° and 44°, corresponding to the graphite structure of CDs with high crystallinity [31,32]. In Fig. 1c, the Raman spectrum shows two peaks at 1544 cm−1 (G band) and 1371 cm−1 (D band) with an intensity ratio I D/I G of 0.91, which indicates that the sp2 graphitic carbon and sp3 carbon defects coexist in CDs [6]. To further investigate the chemical structure of the AA-CDs, the FT-IR spectra of AA-CDs and AA were measured (Fig. 1d). In the FT-IR spectrum of AA, the absorption band at 1710 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching vibration from β-diketone, and the strong absorption peak at 1617 cm−1 is ascribed to the existence of enol tautomer with OC–CC–OH form [33,34]. Whereas, the band at 1710 cm−1 disappears in the FT-IR spectrum of AA-CDs, suggesting that there is no keto form any more in the products; the band at 1606 cm−1 is indicative of the presence of CC double bonds [35]. Besides, the bands at 3600, 1430 and 1050 cm−1 reflect to the O–H, C–H and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively [36–38]. XPS spectra were measured to further confirm the structure of AA-CDs. The survey XPS spectrum shows four main peaks at 1021.8, 531.8, 285.1 and 200.1 eV (Fig. S2a), which can be represented to Zn 2p, O 1s, C 1s and Cl 2p [39–42]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 1e, indicating the existence of CC (284.3 eV), C–C (285 eV), C–OH (286.3 eV) [15,43,44]. The O 1s XPS spectrum shows three peaks at 531.5, 532.3 and 533 eV (Fig. 1f), which can be attributed to CO, C–O and OH respectively [15,43,45]. Cl 2p XPS spectrum in Fig. S2b shows a peak at 199.0 eV, assigned to Zn-Cl [46]. The above results suggest sp2 carbon domain, sp3 carbon defects, Zn doping, surface functional groups in AA-CDs. Furthermore, the above results from TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS characterizations are in good agreement with the proposed formation mechanism of AA-CDs based on Aldol reaction of carbonyl compound AA (Scheme S1).

We systematically investigated the optical properties of the synthesized AA-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, there is a clear absorption peak at 450 nm in the UV absorption spectrum of the CDs. The PL spectrum exhibits a strong green emission peak located at 520 nm. The inset pictures show the photographs of AA-CDs in water taken under day light and 365 nm UV light, respectively. Moreover, compared with common excitation-dependent CDs, the AA-CDs exhibit an excitation-independent PL (Fig. 2b). In a wide range of excitation wavelength from 390 nm to 490 nm, AA-CDs always exhibit green PL with the peak centered at 520 nm. With the excitation wavelength at 440 nm, the PL intensity reaches the maximum. Besides, the time-resolved PL was measured by using time-correlated single-photon counting spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). Similar to those reported previously [6,47], the obtained PL decay curve could be fitted with a biexponential decay function I(t) using equation (1):
(1)
I
( t )
=
A 1
exp
( - t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
( - t /
τ 2
)
where A 1 and A 2 are fractional contributions of PL decay lifetimes τ 1 and τ 2 [48], and the average lifetime (τ) was calculated from equation (2):
(2)
τ
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
The fitting gives the average PL lifetime of 5.49 ns for AA-CDs.

We also explored the effects of different experimental parameters on the absolute PL QY of AA-CDs. To measure the absolute PL QY, an integrating sphere was used [49]. We noted that it was difficult to fabricate fluorescent nanoparticles AA-CDs in the absence of Lewis acid ZnCl2. Besides, in order to achieve the best reaction conditions, we changed the amount of 1, 2-dichloroethane and reaction time separately, keeping other experimental parameters constant. When the reaction time was set as 8 h, the influence of the amount of 1, 2-dichloroethane is shown in Fig. S3a and S3b. We can figure that when the amount of 1, 2-dichloroethane is 0.1 mL, the PL intensity of AA-CDs is the highest and the absolute PL QY reaches 19.2%. Furthermore, by setting the amount of 1, 2-dichloroethane as 0.1 mL, the influence of reaction time is shown in Fig. S3c and Fig. 2d. We can figure that when the reaction time is 9 h, the PL intensity of AA-CDs is the highest, and the absolute PL QY reaches 21% (Fig. S3d). The detailed optical properties of AA-CDs are listed in Table 1 .

Importantly, this mild bottom-up synthesis strategy is universal for the preparation of CDs from various carbonyl compounds. Accordingly, series of CDs with multicolor emission in blue and green regions were synthesized with use of cyclohexanone (CH), 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone (HM), glutaric dialdehyde (GD), and salicylaldehyde (SA) as carbon sources, respectively, and the corresponding CDs are referred to as HM-CDs, CH-CDs, GD-CDs and SA-CDs, respectively (Table 1). In the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (Fig. 3 a–d), they have obvious absorption peaks at 342, 319, 303, and 355 nm, respectively, and show strong emission peaks at 430, 435, 435, and 540 nm, respectively. Under UV light, HM-CDs, CH-CDs, and GD-CDs give blue fluorescence, while SA-CDs present green fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 3e–h, when the excitation wavelength was set as 390 nm, the PL intensity of HM-CDs, CH-CDs and GD-CDs all reaches the highest level, and the PL intensity of SA-CDs reaches the maximum at the excitation of 440 nm. At the same time, as the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peak red-shifts accordingly, showing excitation-dependent PL behavior, which is common in CDs [3,7,12–14]. We explored the effect of reaction temperature on the PL intensity and absolute PL QY of HM-CDs (Fig. S4). When the reaction temperature increases from 50 to 90 °C, the PL intensity and absolute PL QY both increase first and then decrease. The highest absolute PL QY of 16.4% was detected under reaction temperature of 70 °C. In addition, the absolute PL QY of CH-CDs, GD-CDs, and SA-CDs is 17.1%, 15.2%, and 13.1%, respectively. Fig. 3i-l displays the time-resolved PL decay curves of HM-CDs, CH-CDs, GD-CDs and SA-CDs, which could be fitted with the biexponential decay function to give the average fluorescence lifetime of 5.38, 11.02, 11.77, and 3.24 ns, respectively. Generally, the decay is faster in CDs with longer emission wavelength, reflected in decay lifetimes τ 1 and τ 2 (Figs. 2c and Fig. 3i-l). More than one decay components in these cases reveal the complicated radiative pathways possibly originated from the carbon core and the surface state [47,50], while continuous effort is required for the actual PL mechanism of CDs.

For potential application, we gained insight into optical properties of AA-CDs which have highest PL QY in this work, and we found linear relationship between the PL intensity and temperature. AA-CDs were dissolved into water and 2-methyltetrahydrofuran separately, and then we measured the PL intensity under different temperature with the range varying from 280 to 360 K and 150–300 K, respectively (Fig. 4 a and b). Both the aqueous solution and the methyltetrahydrofuran solution of AA-CDs exhibit temperature-dependent PL behavior, where the PL intensity decreases with the increases of temperature. The PL decrease phenomenon may be attributed to enhanced non-radiative recombination with increasing temperature [51]. Fig. 4d show the PL response of AA-CDs towards temperature in the temperature range 280–360 K with linear relationship: I/I0 = −0.00997t + 3.83489 (R2 = 0.96755). Fig. 4e shows the linear regression equation of AA-CDs between I/I0 and temperature in the temperature range 150–300 K: I/I0 = −0.00599t + 1.95067 (R2 = 0.9857). Additionally, the temperature cycle stability of PL intensity of AA-CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4c and f, we tested the PL intensity of AA-CDs at 90 °C and that after cooled down to room temperature. We can find that the PL intensity of AA-CDs almost has no change after 20 cycles, suggesting the good temperature cycle stability of AA-CDs in temperature sensing. In addition, the CDs could be used as phosphors to construct light-emitting devices and as fluorescent ink to fabricate fluorescent patterns by inkjet printing (Fig. S5), showing potentials of these CDs for optoelectronic devices, fluorescent coding, anticounterfeiting, etc.

In conclusion, we developed the bottom-up synthesis of CDs from carbonyl compounds under mild conditions. With use of acetylacetone, cyclohexanone, 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone, glutaric dialdehyde, and salicylaldehyde as carbon sources, five kinds of blue/green-emissive CDs were synthesized in water or THF under moderate conditions (normal pressure, 50~95 °C). As a typical example, the synthesis, characterizations, and optical properties of CDs derived from acetylacetone were thoroughly investigated. The acetylacetone-derived CDs show green emission at 520 nm with an absolute PL QY of 21%. Moreover, the PL intensity decreases proportionally with the increase of temperature in the range 150–360 K, which is expected to be useful for temperature sensing. This work paves a way toward the mild bottom-up preparation of carbon-based nanomaterials with good optical properties.

Cai-Feng Wang and Su Chen supervised the project. Hong-Gang Ye and Xuan Lu performed the experiments, carried out characterizations, and analyzed the data. Rui Cheng, Jiazhuang Guo and He Li contributed to the characterizations. Hong-Gang Ye, Cai-Feng Wang and Su Chen contributed to the interpretation of the results and wrote the paper. All authors reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1602800 and 2016YFB0401700), the National Natural Science Foundation of China(21736006 and 21978132), the Fund of State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (ZK201704 and ZK201716), and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions(PAPD). The authors thank Prof. Haijian Shi at College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University for useful discussion on the formation mechanism of CDs.
